subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can show how farming, manufacturing, and services have different jobs and locations.
Career Research
Your child knows who the leaders are in your town and state and what they do.
Your child can name services that communities offer to people and families.
Your child can name places and services in the community that help children.
Your child can find health information from home, school, and community sources.
Your child knows what makes a good healthcare worker.
Your child understands how to be a responsible healthcare consumer by respecting workers and patients, practicing healthy habits, and speaking up for themselves.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Science: Communities — Services, Careers, and Health
coherence_reason: The standards relate broadly to community roles and services, but some focus on health and healthcare while others address civic leadership or general community services.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can identify ways to get ready for natural disasters.
Your child can name products people use at banks and other financial places.
Your child can name important events and celebrations that happen in your town and in Alabama.
Your child understands how different parts of the brain have special jobs and what happens if those parts get hurt.
Your child can explain different kinds of stores and markets where people shop.
Your child understands that kidneys remove waste from the blood and turn it into urine, which is stored in the bladder.
Your child can recognize the parts of a simple recipe, like measurements, ingredients, directions, and safety tips.
Your child knows how to keep clean and safe when handling food, like washing hands and spotting spoiled food.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Science: Health and Safety — Food Safety and Cleanliness
coherence_reason: The standards cover a wide range of unrelated topics including natural disasters, financial literacy, community events, brain and kidney functions, types of stores, and food safety, so they should be split.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child knows the Sun is the biggest object in the solar system and is mostly made of hydrogen and helium.
Your child knows that a planet moves around the Sun because of the Sun’s gravity pulling on it.
Your child knows the solar system includes Earth, the Moon, the Sun, eight other planets with their moons, and smaller objects like asteroids and comets.
Your child knows heat can move between objects through radiation, which can travel through space.
Your child can explain that stars are different sizes and brightness, and all stars except the Sun look like tiny points of light because they are far away.
Your child understands that plants need light to grow.
Your child understands that things that give off light often give off heat too.
Distinguish light and dark.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Science: Space Systems — Sun, Stars, and the Solar System
coherence_reason: The standards cluster around the solar system and the Sun, but also include unrelated concepts such as plant growth, heat transfer, and distinguishing light and dark.
split_topics: Light and heat (energy transfer, plant needs)

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can find and try out ways to fix simple computer or device problems.
Your child can find simple solutions to fix common computer hardware and software problems.
Your child can find and fix simple computer problems like power or connection issues during everyday use.
Your child can explain and try simple steps to fix a basic technology problem.
Your child finds out why a product or system is not working properly.
Your child can figure out why a product or system is not working right.
Your child can use problem-solving steps to fix issues with computer hardware and software.
Troubleshooting
canonical_name: Grade 3 Science: Engineering Design — Troubleshooting Simple Technologies
coherence_reason: All standards refer to students identifying, explaining, and solving simple problems with computers, devices, or systems.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child will use pictures, music, or videos that are free to use, and will not copy things made by others without permission.
Your child can explain the choices they made while creating a computer program, using notes, presentations, or demonstrations.
Your child will take on different jobs, with help from the teacher, when working with classmates to design, build, and review computer programs.
Your child can break big problems into smaller steps to help create a computer program.
Your child works well with others in group activities.
Your child can make computer programs that use steps in order, respond to events, repeat actions, and make decisions.
Your child will ask for ideas from different people to help make computer projects better.
Your child can find and fix mistakes in a computer program to make sure it works correctly.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Science: Computer Science — Collaboration, Problem-Solving, and Programming Basics
coherence_reason: The cluster includes both programming concepts (breaking down problems, debugging, sequencing, events, loops, conditionals) and collaborative practices (working in groups, seeking feedback, respecting copyright), which are related but represent two distinct sub-concepts.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child will think of ways to make technology easier to use for people with different needs and preferences.
Your child can use different strategies to read and understand informational texts smoothly.
Your child will research places like museums, libraries, and online sites to learn about jobs in science, technology, engineering, and math.
Your child can use old documents, letters, diaries, maps, and photos to learn about history.
Your child can figure out the meaning of unknown or tricky words using different strategies while reading or learning.
Your child can suggest ways to make technology easier and better for different users.
Your child can write, share, and publish work using technology tools.
Digital tools
canonical_name: Grade 3 Science: Technology — Accessibility and Use
coherence_reason: The standards mix technology accessibility, digital tool use, and research on STEM careers, along with unrelated reading comprehension and historical document analysis.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can show how computer parts and programs work together to get things done.
Your child can explain how the inside and outside parts of a computer work together as a team.
Your child can show how information is split into small pieces, sent through the internet, and put back together at the end.
Your child can describe how computers connect to other parts to work as a system.
Your child can show how information is broken into small parts, sent through devices on the internet, and put back together at the end.
Your child can show how computer parts and programs work together to complete tasks.
Your child knows that computers can act smart in things like robots, speech recognition, and animations.
Your child can explain what different computer parts do, like the screen, keyboard, or speakers.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Science: Computers — Parts, Systems, and Information Flow
coherence_reason: The standards cover both computer hardware/system interactions and the specifics of information transmission over networks, which are related but distinct concepts.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can explain how buying and selling have changed because of the global economy.
Your child can explain how industrial growth, city life, new communication, and cultural changes affected life in the U.S. from the late 1800s to World War I.
Your child can describe how people worked to improve society and change unfair conditions during the Progressive Era.
Your child can talk about how computers and technology have changed the world and how they are connected to different cultures.
Your child can describe the cultures, governments, and economies of American Indian groups in Alabama, both long ago and more recently.
Your child can explain how European exploration changed life for both Europeans and American Indians during the Age of Discovery.
Your child can identify important cultural and economic changes in the United States from 1900 to the 1930s.
Your child can tell about important people from different backgrounds who helped your community and state, both long ago and today.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Social Studies: United States History — Social and Economic Change
coherence_reason: The standards cover a wide range of historical, economic, cultural, and technological changes over different time periods, and also reference local and national history as well as the global economy.
split_topics: Historical Changes in the United States (Industrialization, Progressive Era, Age of Discovery, 1900-1930s)
Impact of Technology and Global Economy
Cultural and Economic Aspects of American Indian Groups
Contributions of Important People to Community and State

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can watch and measure how things move to find patterns that help predict future motion.
Your child uses spelling patterns, word meanings, and how words relate to each other to read new words and learn new vocabulary.
Your child can group objects using two things they notice, like size and shape or color and texture.
Your child can watch and explain ways to stop something from falling, like catching a ball.
Your child can notice when an object starts moving or changes how it moves.
Your child knows that science discoveries come from noticing patterns.
Your child can ask questions that can be answered by looking for cause and effect patterns.
Your child can use words like first, next, and then to show the order of events in writing.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Science: Motion and Patterns — Observing, Measuring, and Predicting Movement
coherence_reason: Most standards refer to observing, measuring, and analyzing physical motion and patterns, but a few (related to vocabulary and writing) are unrelated to the main topic.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child understands energy can move from one place to another by heat, waves like water, light, and sound, or by moving objects.
Your child knows that when fuel burns, most of the energy released turns into heat.
Your child knows that cellular respiration breaks down food and oxygen to release energy needed for body functions like keeping warm.
Your child knows uneven heating of Earth causes air to move in currents.
Your child can recognize examples of energy use, like electricity powering a radio or motion moving a bowling ball.
Your child understands different kinds of energy, like light, heat, electricity, and motion.
Your child can observe and tell that rubbing objects together produces heat.
Your child knows some objects use electricity, like a TV, and some use movement energy, like a bowling ball.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Science: Energy — Forms, Transfer, and Uses
coherence_reason: Most standards address forms of energy and their transfer or use, though some focus on specific processes (cellular respiration, Earth's heating) that could be considered more advanced or distinct applications.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can explain how different things or conditions can make objects, living things, or systems stay the same or change.
Your child knows water vapor moves through the air, forms fog or clouds, and falls as rain, hail, sleet, or snow.
Your child understands heat moves through solids by conduction and through fluids by conduction and convection, which involves movement of the fluid.
Your child knows that during chemical reactions, atoms rearrange to make new substances with different properties.
Your child knows that metals share traits like conducting electricity and heat, and that some metals are pure while others are mixtures.
Your child understands that when water evaporates, it becomes vapor in the air and can turn back into liquid or ice when cooled.
Your child understands that all matter is made of atoms, which can join together to form molecules.
Your child knows that ice can melt and turn into water.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Science: Matter — States, Changes, and Properties
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on the properties, states, and changes of matter, though some include broader concepts like chemical reactions and properties of metals.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can plan and do an experiment to show how balanced and unbalanced forces change how things move.
Your child can ask questions to find out how electric or magnetic forces affect objects without touching.
Your child can describe a simple problem that can be solved using ideas about magnets.
Your child learns how things move and stay still because of forces pushing or pulling.
Your child understands that air pressure from Earth’s atmosphere gets weaker as you go higher and pushes equally in all directions at any spot.
Your child can notice when an object changes how it moves.
Your child knows that something can be stopped from falling.
Your child can investigate and explain that rubbing one object against another makes heat, like rubbing hands together.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Science: Forces and Motion — Pushes, Pulls, and Effects on Objects
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on forces and motion, but some address properties of air pressure and heat from friction, which are related but distinct sub-concepts.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can organize and show data in charts or pictures to explain their thinking or prove a point.
Your child can describe the world using maps and other geography tools.
Your child will gather and combine information to describe different climates around the world.
Your child uses strategies to read smoothly and understand different kinds of stories and books.
Your child can show weather data in tables and graphs to explain what weather is usually like in a season.
Your child can use math skills to understand how much borrowing costs.
Your child can find important lines on maps and globes like the prime meridian, equator, Tropics of Capricorn and Cancer, International Date Line, and lines of latitude and longitude.
Your child can use calendars and timelines to learn about events from the past.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Science: Data Analysis — Organizing and Representing Weather Data
coherence_reason: The standards include some about data analysis and weather, but several are about geography, reading, economics, and history, making this a poorly grouped cluster.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can create models to show that all living things have life stages like birth, growth, reproduction, and death.
Your child can create and use models or simple designs to show how things work or to solve problems.
Your child can use the ideas of supply and demand to understand buying goods and services.
Your child can identify and draw shapes by their features, like how many angles or equal sides they have, including triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons, and cubes.
Your child can identify that models represent real-world things.
Your child can recognize when a model shows a real object.
Your child can build a simple model of something like a flower or the solar system and explain what it shows and what it doesn’t.
Your child understands that models stand for real things.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Science: Scientific Models — Representation of Real-World Objects and Processes
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on understanding and using models to represent real objects or processes, though one standard is about economic concepts and another about geometry, which are unrelated.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can explain if a solution is good for helping plants and animals when their environment changes.
Your child can explain why a design helps reduce problems caused by bad weather.
Your child finds and shares information about how well current solutions reduce damage from weather hazards.
Your child can explain money goals based on what is important to them.
Your child can explain why a design helps reduce problems caused by weather hazards.
Your child can explain why a solution to a problem is good by giving evidence about how it meets the problem’s goals and limits.
Your child can explain why a solution to a problem is good by using evidence about how it fits the problem's needs.
Cause and Effect
canonical_name: Grade 3 Science: Engineering Solutions — Evaluating Designs for Environmental Challenges
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on evaluating solutions to environmental and weather-related problems, but one standard about 'money goals' does not fit and the reference to 'Cause and Effect' is generic.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child understands real-life problems with online safety and knows ways to keep personal information private.
Your child uses the right kind of language when talking in both casual and formal situations.
Your child can tell the difference between credit products and services.
Describe creditworthiness.
Your child understands what rights people have and what they should do as citizens in your community and state.
Your child knows that contracts are promises people must keep.
Your child understands why we pay taxes and what responsibilities come with them.
Your child can recognize interest rates, fees, and other charges related to payments.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Social Studies: Financial Literacy — Credit, Contracts, and Taxes
coherence_reason: The cluster includes financial literacy concepts (credit, contracts, taxes), but also contains standards about online safety, appropriate language, and citizenship responsibilities, which are distinct sub-concepts.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can explain with evidence why some animals live well in a habitat, some live less well, and some cannot live there at all.
Your child can explain why some animals live in groups to help each other survive.
Your child understands how differences in animals or plants help them survive, find mates, and have babies.
Your child knows that species can die out when their environment changes and they cannot adapt well enough to survive.
Your child can sort animals into big groups like mammals, birds, or reptiles by how they look and act.
Your child can group animals by similar features, like fur, feathers, or number of legs.
Your child can sort common animals by how they look.
Your child can match animals that look the same.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Science: Life Science — Animal Adaptations, Classification, and Survival
coherence_reason: The standards cover two related but distinct sub-concepts: animal adaptation/survival and animal classification by features.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can come up with and compare several possible solutions to a problem to see which ones best meet the goals and limits.
Your child can describe a simple design problem based on a need or want, including what success looks like and limits like materials, time, or cost.
Your child can compare different types of economies (like traditional, command, market, and mixed), and explain what makes each one good or bad.
Your child can look at different ways to solve a problem with computer instructions and pick the best one.
Your child can explain ways to reach money goals.
Identify sources of credit.
Your child can compare different financial services and products to see which fit their goals.
Your child understands that people decide how to spend money based on what they have, what they need, and what they want.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Science: Engineering Design — Defining Problems and Evaluating Solutions
coherence_reason: Cluster contains both science/engineering design standards and economics/financial literacy standards, which are clearly different topics.
split_topics: Engineering Design — Defining Problems and Evaluating Solutions
Economics and Financial Literacy — Economic Systems and Decision Making

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can plan and do fair tests by controlling variables and thinking about what might go wrong to find ways to improve a model or prototype.
Your child knows that scientists ask questions, talk about, and check each other’s ideas and evidence.
Your child can work with a group to watch and figure out results.
Your child can ask simple questions, explore, watch carefully, and share what they learn.
Your child asks simple questions, explores, observes, and shares what they learn.
Your child understands that scientists use information from observations and measurements to explain how things happen in nature.
Your child can help with science activities when working with a partner.
Your child asks questions about nature, explores and investigates alone or with others, and explains what they find out.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Science: Scientific Inquiry — Asking Questions and Investigations
coherence_reason: All standards address the process of scientific inquiry, including asking questions, conducting investigations, making observations, working collaboratively, and sharing findings.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can explain how new science discoveries and inventions affect our world and daily life.
Engineering Design
Your child understands that scientists need to communicate and share ideas to learn more.
Your child understands how models and simulations help solve real-world science and engineering problems.
Your child can use thinking skills to solve real science and engineering problems.
Your child knows the meaning of science words like telescope, environment, and solid.
Your child knows the meanings of science words like telescope, environment, and solid.
Your child understands how thinking like a computer scientist can help solve real-life science and engineering problems.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Science: Engineering and Scientific Practice — Problem Solving and Communication
coherence_reason: Standards address related aspects of scientific and engineering practice (problem solving, modeling, communication), but also include vocabulary knowledge which is a distinct subtopic.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can explain what caused World War I, what happened because of it, and why the United States joined the war.
Your child can use information to show or guess what causes what, make predictions, or explain ideas.
Your child can explain their opinion about a community issue and give a reason for it.
Your child can explain reasons why people borrow money and go into debt.
Your child can use information from data to find connections, guess what might happen next, or explain ideas.
Your child can record what they observe using writing or pictures.
Your child can write or draw observations to explain what they found.
Your child can write or draw observations to explain what they found, like using picture stories.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Science: Scientific Investigation — Recording and Communicating Observations
coherence_reason: Some standards are about scientific observation and recording, but others are social studies or general skills (cause/effect, opinions, borrowing money), so this cluster contains at least two distinct sub-concepts.
split_topics: Cause and effect, opinion, and social studies content

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child understands how regions help organize and describe Earth's surface.
Your child can study fossils to learn about animals and environments from long ago.
Your child recognizes what the Declaration of Independence and the U.S. Constitution do for our country.
Your child understands how saving money and investing money are connected.
Your child can identify how people use natural resources, work, and tools to make goods and provide services.
Your child can identify reasons why people moved to and settled in North America and what happened because of it.
Your child understands how culture, like religion and food, helps define different regions.
Your child understands how American Indian cultures in North America were different based on where they lived, their resources, communities, economies, and beliefs.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Social Studies: Geography and Culture — Regions, Resources, and Communities
coherence_reason: The standards mix geography, culture, civics, economics, and history, which are distinct subtopics within social studies.
split_topics: Migration and settlement in North America
Culture and regional differences (religion, food, American Indians)

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can look at data to show that plants and animals get traits from their parents and that these traits can vary.
Your child can use evidence to show how the environment can change traits in living things.
Your child can study how populations grow, spread out, and change over time.
Your child knows that many living things have special parts that help move materials around their bodies.
Your child knows that both genes and the environment affect how traits appear and how common they are in a group.
Your child learns how traits are passed from parents to children and how they can vary.
Your child knows the steps of digestion and how the mouth, esophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, and colon each help break down food.
Your child learns about the parts of plants and animals and how they help them live and grow.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Science: Life Science — Traits, Heredity, and Structure
coherence_reason: While most standards address heredity and variation of traits, several focus on organism structure and function, especially digestive and transport systems, indicating two related but distinct sub-concepts.
split_topics: Structure and Function of Living Things
